books.title: Everyone Else Looks Fine
books.subtitle: When the whole feed seems to have it together and you're the only one falling behind — and how to step off the scoreboard
books.desc: Scroll, and everyone seems to have it together — calmer, smarter, further ahead. This is a book about the comparing that runs all day and convinces you you're the only one behind.
books.long_desc:
Open the feed and there they all are. Calmer than you. Further ahead. Posting the grade, the win, the friend group, the life that looks easy. And here you are — behind again, sure you missed some memo everyone else got, certain you're the only one this is hard for.

Your fast brain is good at a lot of things, and one of them is comparison. It ranks. It scans the room, the group chat, the feed, looking for where you stand — and it always, always puts you near the bottom. Not because that's true. Because that's what a scared brain does with other people: it turns them into a scoreboard.

This book is about that scoreboard, and how it lies. You're measuring the inside of your own head — every doubt, every 2am spiral, every thing you didn't say — against the outside of everyone else. Their highlight reel. The five seconds they chose to show. It was never a fair fight, and it was never even a real one.

You'll learn to catch the comparison the instant it starts, that reflexive ranking you do without deciding to. You'll learn that "everyone else is fine" is a story you're writing, not a fact you found, and that the calm you imagine in other people is mostly just the part they didn't post.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the whole point. There's no scoreboard, no one to beat, no memo you missed. There's just you, allowed to stop ranking yourself against people whose insides you will never actually see.

Book 6 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're comparing your insides to their highlight reel.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: It's Not an Emergency
books.subtitle: When your body hits the panic button over nothing — and you start to learn the difference
books.desc: Your heart slams, your chest goes tight, and your whole body is sure something is wrong — except nothing is. This is a book about the false alarm that goes off when there's no fire.
books.long_desc:
Your body just hit the panic button. Heart going, chest tight, that full-body certainty that something is very wrong — and you look around and nothing is. No fire. No threat. Just you, a glowing phone, and an alarm that won't shut off.

You're not broken and you're not dramatic. You've got an alarm system that fires fast and fires hard, and lately it goes off over a text you haven't answered, a grade that isn't posted yet, a tomorrow that hasn't happened. It's trying to protect you. It's just really, really bad at telling a real emergency from a fake one.

This book is about learning the difference. Not by forcing yourself to calm down — that never works and you know it — but by getting close enough to the alarm to feel what it actually is. A signal, not a sentence. Loud, but not always right.

You'll start to catch the moment the panic switches on, before it convinces you the sky is falling. You'll learn that a racing heart is not proof of danger, that "I feel like something's wrong" is a feeling and not a fact, and that you can let the alarm ring without obeying every word it says.

Nothing here will make the alarm disappear. That's not the goal. The goal is that the next time it goes off over nothing, some quiet part of you knows it — and you don't have to run.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your alarm is loud. It isn't always right.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The Quiet Underneath
books.subtitle: The loss hiding inside the worry — mourning the easy version of yourself you thought you'd be
books.desc: Under the racing and the worry, sometimes there's something heavier and quieter — a sadness for the easy version of yourself you thought you'd be. This is a book about the grief hiding inside anxiety.
books.long_desc:
Sometimes, underneath all the noise, there's something the worry was covering. Not fear. Something heavier and slower. A quiet you can't quite name.

It's grief. Not the kind anyone holds a funeral for — the kind nobody tells you to expect. You're grieving the version of yourself who was supposed to have an easy time of it. The you who'd feel things less, who wouldn't lie awake, who'd move through school and friends and growing up without a brain that turns everything to alarm. You watch people who seem to get the easy version, and something in you mourns the one you didn't.

This book makes room for that. Most anxiety advice never does — it's all about managing, optimizing, getting better, moving on. But you can't manage a loss you won't let yourself feel, and so much of the racing is just grief that never got to be sad out loud.

You'll learn to tell the difference between fear, which wants you to fight, and grief, which only ever wanted to be felt. You'll learn that mourning the easy version isn't giving up — it's what lets you finally meet the actual you, the one who's here, the one whose brain runs fast and feels deep and was never the consolation prize.

The sadness won't vanish on a schedule; grief doesn't work that way, and this book won't pretend otherwise. But once it's named, it stops having to disguise itself as panic. And what's underneath the quiet, it turns out, is something a lot like tenderness — for the you that's actually here.

Book 5 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Some anxiety is grief that never got named.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Too Many Tabs Open
books.subtitle: When everything is due at once and your brain just freezes — and how to find the one next thing
books.desc: Five assignments, three group chats, a brain with forty tabs open and not one of them loading. This is a book about the overwhelm that freezes you right when you need to move.
books.long_desc:
Everything is due. Everything is happening. And your brain has somehow responded by doing absolutely nothing.

You know the feeling. The list is so long you can't pick a starting point. There are too many tabs open in your head and behind your eyes, all of them blinking, none of them loading. People say "just break it into small steps," like you hadn't thought of that, like the problem was that you forgot. The problem isn't the steps. The problem is that when it all comes at once, your fast brain floods — and a flooded brain can't even find the first thing.

This book isn't another system to organize your life. You've tried systems. This is about what actually happens in your head when too much lands at once, and why freezing isn't laziness or failure — it's what an overloaded circuit does.

You'll learn to feel the flood coming before it takes the whole screen. You'll learn that you were never supposed to hold all of it at the same time, that "everything" is not a real size your brain can pick up, and that the way out is almost embarrassingly small: one thing. Not the right thing. Just one.

There's no calm productivity hack waiting at the end of this. The to-do list will still be long. But you'll stop confusing a loud, crowded head with an actual emergency — and you'll know how to find the one next thing when the whole screen lights up.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Everything at once is the same as nothing.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The Part That Says You're the Problem
books.subtitle: When the anxiety stops being about the thing and starts being about you — and why it's lying
books.desc: There's a voice underneath the worry that says the problem isn't the test or the text — it's you. This is a book about the shame hiding inside the anxiety, and why it's lying.
books.long_desc:
Somewhere under the worry, there's a quieter voice. It doesn't say "this is hard." It says "what is wrong with you." It says everyone else can do this, that you're too much and not enough at the same time, that something in you is wrong in a way that can't be undone.

That voice feels like the truth. It feels like the most honest thing in your head. But it isn't truth — it's shame, and shame is the cruelest trick anxiety plays, because it stops being about the test or the text or the room full of people and quietly becomes about you. The thing you are. The thing it swears you can't change.

This book goes after that voice directly. Not to argue with it — you can't win an argument with shame, it changes the subject every time — but to see where it actually came from, how it learned to sound like you, and why a brain that runs fast and feels everything loud is so easy to convince that it's broken.

You'll learn to hear the difference between "I did a thing badly" and "I am bad," because shame blurs those on purpose. You'll learn that the voice swearing it's all you is the same voice in everyone who's ever felt too different, and that being hard on yourself was never the thing that made you better — it just made you quieter.

The voice may not vanish. It's had a lot of practice. But you'll stop mistaking it for your own, and you'll stop letting it have the last word about who you are.

Book 4 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: The voice swears it's you. It's wrong.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: One Thought Pulls the Next
books.subtitle: How a single worry turns into a 2am downward loop — and how to step off the slide
books.desc: It starts with one small worry and forty minutes later you're convinced your whole life is falling apart. This is a book about the spiral — how it works, and how to step off the slide.
books.long_desc:
It always starts so small. One thought. A thing you said, a thing you didn't, a what-if you can't quite drop. And then the next thought grabs the first one's hand, and the next grabs that one, and somehow forty minutes later you're lying in the dark certain that everyone hates you and your whole life is wrong.

That's the spiral. It's not you being irrational. It's a fast brain doing what fast brains do — connecting everything to everything, at speed, in the worst possible direction, usually at 2am when there's nothing left to interrupt it.

This is the longest book in the series, because the spiral needs room. You can't outrun it with a tip. You have to understand how it actually moves: how one true-feeling thought becomes a chain, how the chain feels like proof, how the bottom of the slide always seems like the truth even though it's just the bottom of the slide.

You'll learn to feel the first link before the chain locks. You'll learn that a thought arriving fast and feeling huge is not the same as a thought being true, that you do not have to follow every thread your brain throws, and that the loop, however convincing, is a loop — which means it goes nowhere, which means you can step off.

The spiral may still start. Probably it will; that's how your brain is wired, and this book won't rewire it. But you'll know the slide for what it is. And knowing is the first thing that ever slowed it down.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: It started with one thought. It never stops there.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The One Watching You
books.subtitle: The part of you that monitors every move, every word, every face in the room — and how to let it rest
books.desc: There's a part of you that never stops watching — tracking your own face, your words, how you're coming across, every second. This is a book about the watcher, and how to finally let it rest.
books.long_desc:
You're never just in the room. There's a part of you up in the corner, watching — clocking your own face, replaying what you just said, scanning everyone else for how it landed. It narrates you to yourself all day. Did that sound weird. Are they looking. Why did you do that with your hands.

That's the watcher. It's the most exhausting part of an anxious, fast-running brain, because it never clocks out. It turns living into a performance you're always grading, and it's convinced that if it ever stopped monitoring, you'd fall apart, embarrass yourself, get caught being whatever it is you're afraid of being.

This is the last book in the series, and it's the one underneath all the others — because the false alarm, the spiral, the shame, the comparing, they all need the watcher to keep score. So this book goes to the source. Not to kill the watcher; it started as something trying to keep you safe. But to show it that you're not in danger, and that it's allowed to put the camera down.

You'll learn to notice the watching while it's happening, that strange split where you're both living and filming. You'll learn that self-consciousness is the watcher doing its job, not proof that anything's wrong, and that the people around you are far too busy watching themselves to be running the tape on you.

The watcher won't fully sign off; it's been on the job too long, and this book won't fire it. But it can learn to rest. And in the quiet when it finally does, you might notice something you've been too watched to feel in a long time: you, just here, not performing — allowed to simply be in the room.

Book 7 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're not just in the room. You're filming it.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Everyone Else Has It Figured Out
books.subtitle: For when the feed says you're behind and you believe it every time
books.desc: Scroll for thirty seconds and everyone else seems further ahead, more sure, already there. This is a book about the comparing that never lets you feel like enough — and how little it was ever telling you the truth.
books.long_desc:
Open the app and there it is again. Someone got in. Someone's project blew up. Someone is calm and finished and clearly figured something out that you didn't.

And there you are, half-done and unsure, certain you're the only one still figuring it out. It's not that you're not trying. It's that everyone else seems to be trying less and getting more — like they were handed a head start you never got, running a race you didn't know had already started. So you measure. Constantly. Where you rank, who's ahead, how far behind you must be. And the measuring never once makes you feel like enough.

This is a book for the mind that can't stop comparing. The one that turns every feed, every grade, every group chat into a scoreboard with you somewhere near the bottom. You're not shallow and you're not jealous. You've just got a really sensitive instrument pointed at exactly the wrong thing — other people's outsides, stacked against your insides.

You'll learn to catch the comparison as it happens — the quick math, the sinking feeling, the certainty that everyone else is fine. And you'll learn what that highlight reel never shows: that the calm you're imagining in everyone else is a story you're writing, and the head start you think you missed was never real.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the whole point. There's no scoreboard that was ever telling the truth, no one you actually need to beat. There's just the slow relief of looking up from the feed and realizing the race was never real — and you're allowed to stop running it.

The comparing voice might still chime in. But you can stop letting it tell you where you stand.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Nobody is as far ahead as your feed makes them look.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: If They Really Knew Me
books.subtitle: For when you're sure you faked your way here and they're about to find out
books.desc: You're sure that if people saw the real you, they'd stop being impressed. This is a book about that secret, sinking feeling that you're a fraud — and the slow relief of being seen anyway.
books.long_desc:
There's a version of you that everyone sees. And then there's the one you keep behind your back.

The first one has the grades, the answers, the project that turned out fine. The second one knows it was luck, or faking, or staying up until 2am so nobody would see how hard it actually was. And you carry this quiet, sinking certainty: if they ever saw the real me — the one who doesn't get it, the one who's barely holding it together — they'd be so disappointed. So you keep performing. You get good at it. And it never once makes the feeling go away.

This is a book for the part of you that's sure you're a fraud. Not the nervous, butterflies kind — the heavier kind. The hot-face, look-away, please-don't-look-too-close kind. The feeling that there's something wrong with you specifically, and everyone else got the manual you missed.

You'll learn to notice that feeling the moment it shows up — the flinch when someone praises you, the urge to explain it away, the exhausting work of managing what people think. And you'll learn something strange and true: the shame was never proof that you're broken. It was just proof that you care a lot, in a world that taught you caring should be hidden.

Nothing here is going to make you more impressive. That's not the point. There's no real you to fix and no fake you to maintain — there's just you, the whole thing, finally allowed in the room. You don't have to earn your spot by hiding the parts you're ashamed of.

The voice that says "if they really knew me" might still whisper. But you can stop believing it knows the first thing about you.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: The realest part of you is the part you hide.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: False Alarm
books.subtitle: Why your brain keeps screaming emergency over things that turn out fine — and how to tell loud from true
books.desc: You sent the text an hour ago and your brain is still treating it like a five-alarm emergency. This is a book about the alarm that keeps going off when there was never any fire.
books.long_desc:
You sent the message. It was fine. You know it was fine.

So why is your whole body acting like something is on fire? Your chest is tight, your brain is replaying it on a loop, and some part of you is dead certain you ruined everything — over a text, a comment in class, a face someone made that probably meant nothing. The alarm in your head is screaming, and you've been scrambling to put out a fire that was never actually there.

Here's the thing nobody told you: that alarm isn't proof. It's a smoke detector that goes off when you make toast. Loud, sure. Convincing, definitely. But loud and true are not the same thing, and your brain has been confusing them for years.

This book isn't going to teach you to rip the alarm out of the wall. You can't, and honestly you wouldn't want to — it's trying to look out for you. What you'll learn instead is how to recognize a false alarm while it's blaring. To feel that jolt of "something is wrong" and actually ask, for once, wrong how? To notice the gap between what your body is yelling and what is actually happening in the room.

You'll start to catch the pattern: the spike, the certainty, the scramble — and then nothing. No fire. Again. And every time you see the alarm for what it is, it gets a little easier to hear it without believing every word.

Nothing in here will make you stop caring. You'll still feel things loudly; that's just how you're wired. But you can stop treating every alarm like the building's coming down. You can learn the difference between a real fire and burnt toast — and finally stop running for the exit over nothing.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your head pulled the alarm. Nothing's burning.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: One Thought Deep
books.subtitle: How a single worry pulls the next one in until you're somewhere you never meant to go
books.desc: It started as one small worry. Twenty minutes later you're three years into the future, certain everything falls apart. This is a book about the spiral — and how to catch it before it pulls you all the way down.
books.long_desc:
It always starts so small.

One thought. A tiny "what if." And if you'd caught it right there, it would've been nothing. But you didn't, and that thought reached back and grabbed the next one, and that one grabbed another, and now it's twenty minutes later and somehow you've gone from "did that come out weird" to "everyone secretly can't stand me and my whole life is going to fall apart." You didn't decide to go there. The spiral took you.

That's the part that makes it feel impossible. It's not one big scary thought you could just argue with. It's a hundred small ones, each one perfectly reasonable, linking into a chain that drags you somewhere awful one calm-sounding step at a time.

This book is about the spiral itself — the way it moves, the trapdoors it uses, the exact moment it goes from thinking to falling. You'll learn to feel the floor start to tilt. To notice the link being made — this thought reaching for the next — before the chain locks in and the whole thing picks up speed.

Because here's what's true: you don't have to fight your way back up a spiral once you're at the bottom. That almost never works. What works is catching the second link, the third — the place where it's still just two thoughts holding hands and not a free fall. You'll get better at spotting that exact spot. Earlier and earlier, until catching it starts to feel almost normal.

You're not broken for going there. A mind that links fast is a mind that's quick and alive — it just runs downhill when no one's steering. This won't flatten that mind out. It'll hand you the moment of choice the spiral has been stealing from you.

One thought is just one thought. You can learn to leave it at that.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: It started with one thought. Then it didn't stop.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: The Watcher in Your Head
books.subtitle: On living every moment twice — once as you, once as the person watching you do it
books.desc: There's a part of you watching you — narrating how you look, sound, and land, every second. This is a book about the watcher in your head, and what happens when you finally catch it watching.
books.long_desc:
You're never just in the room.

There's a part of you up in the corner, watching. Watching how you stand, how your voice sounded, whether that laugh came out right. It narrates you to yourself in real time — too much, too weird, why did you say that — like there's a camera on you that never cuts. You live every moment twice: once as the person living it, and once as the person watching, judging, taking notes.

No wonder you're exhausted. Being your own audience is a full-time job, and the watcher never claps. It only critiques.

This book is about that part of you — where it came from, what it thinks it's protecting you from, and why it turned the camera around on you in the first place. Because the watcher isn't a villain. Somewhere back there it decided that if it caught every flaw before anyone else did, you'd be safe. It's been filming ever since, certain that's the only thing keeping you okay.

You'll learn to notice the watching as it happens — to catch the camera mid-pan. To feel the difference between actually being in a moment and standing outside it grading yourself. And here's the strange relief in it: you can't be the watcher and fully be yourself at the same time. The second you see the watcher watching, you're already back in your own body, in the actual room, where life was happening the whole time.

This won't make you stop caring how you come across. But it'll loosen the watcher's grip enough that you get to live some of your life from the inside — not from the corner of the ceiling, taking notes on yourself.

You were never meant to be your own surveillance. You were meant to be here.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're not just here. You're watching yourself be here.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: Everyone Else Has It Figured Out
books.subtitle: Why scrolling makes you feel so far behind — and how to stop measuring your whole life against everyone's highlight reel
books.desc: Everyone in the feed is promoted, engaged, thriving, sorted — and you're still behind, still struggling with stuff they make look easy. This is a book about the comparison that runs every time you open your phone.
books.long_desc:
You open your phone for a second and close it feeling like a failure.

Everyone's announcing something. The promotion, the apartment, the relationship, the side project that's somehow taking off. They all look so sorted — calm, capable, ten steps ahead — while you're back here struggling with the basic stuff they make look effortless. The gap feels enormous and it feels like proof: everyone else got it figured out, and you, specifically, did not.

This is a book for the mind that turns every feed into a scoreboard and always ranks itself last. The one trained since childhood to measure up, to read the room for who's ahead, to scroll and tally and come away certain you're behind in a race nobody actually started. You're not lazy and you're not failing. You've just aimed a brutally sensitive comparison instrument at the one target it can never read fairly: yourself, against a thousand strangers' best moments.

You'll learn to catch comparison the instant it fires — the scroll, the clench, the silent ranking — and to feel how little it has ever told you that was actually true. You'll learn that the togetherness you're seeing is a highlight reel, edited and posted, and that the secret you think everyone else has was never the thing you were missing.

Nothing here will help you win the comparison. That's the point. There's no position to optimize, no one to finally catch — and the brains that look the calmest are running their own loud alarms you'll never see. The shift is quieter: recognizing the race as the thing hurting you, and letting yourself step off the scoreboard long enough to live your actual life.

The comparing mind may still speak up every time you scroll. But you can stop letting a feed decide how far behind you are.

Book 6 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: They're not actually fine. Neither is the feed.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: False Alarm
books.subtitle: Why your body keeps sounding the alarm when nothing's actually wrong — and how to finally tell the difference
books.desc: Your body hits the panic button over a Slack ping, a left-on-read text, a task you haven't started. This is a book about the alarm that goes off when there's no fire — and learning, at last, to tell real danger from noise.
books.long_desc:
Your heart is pounding. Your chest is tight. Something is wrong — your whole body is sure of it.

Then you check: the Slack message was fine. The text didn't mean anything. The task isn't even due. Nothing is actually on fire. But the alarm in your body went off like the building was burning, and now you're shaky and wired and a little embarrassed, because there was nothing there. Again.

This is a book for the brain that came with the alarm cranked all the way up. The one that treats a notification like a threat, a tone of voice like a verdict, an unanswered email like proof you're in trouble. You're not dramatic and you're not broken. Your wiring just fires loud and fires fast — and it has never once stopped to check whether the danger was real.

You'll learn to feel the alarm the second it starts — the chest, the held breath, the hand reaching for the phone — and to recognize it for what it is before it runs the whole afternoon. You'll learn that the feeling of danger and the fact of danger are two different things, and that your body has been confusing them your entire life.

Nothing here will make the alarm go away. That's not the goal, and anyone who promises it is selling you something. The goal is quieter and more useful: to know the sound well enough that it stops fooling you. To feel your body shout fire and stay long enough to see there's no smoke.

The alarm will still go off. But you can stop treating every false alarm like the real thing.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your chest is screaming. Nothing's on fire.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The Years It Took
books.subtitle: The quiet grief underneath the anxiety — mourning the time, the energy, and the version of you it kept taking
books.desc: Under the anxiety is something nobody warns you about: grief — for the hours it ate, the chances it cost, the easier life you keep picturing. This is a book about letting yourself actually mourn it.
books.long_desc:
There's a feeling underneath the anxiety that almost nobody names. It isn't fear. It's grief.

Grief for the hours it took — the afternoons lost to a frozen screen, the nights eaten by a spiral, the energy spent just keeping yourself upright. Grief for the chances you didn't take because your brain talked you out of them. Grief for the easier, lighter version of your life you can picture so clearly and have never once gotten to live. It catches you sideways, this feeling, and you usually push it down, because what's the use. But it's real, and it's been waiting.

This is a book for the person carrying a loss they've never let themselves call a loss. The one who's quietly furious or quietly sad about everything an anxious, wired brain has cost them — and who's been told to focus on coping, on gratitude, on moving forward, when something in them just needs to grieve first. You're not being dramatic. You lost real things. The fact that no one held a service for them doesn't make them less gone.

You'll learn to feel the grief as its own distinct thing, separate from the anxiety that caused it — to stop rushing past it toward the next fix and let yourself actually mourn what got taken. You'll learn why this loss is so hard to name, why it keeps arriving uninvited, and what it asks of you when it does.

Nothing here will give those years back. That's the hard, honest center of this one — there's no reframe that returns lost time, and you deserve better than to be told there is. What's possible is quieter and more humane: to let the loss be real, to feel it all the way through, and to find that grieving it is what finally lets you stop carrying it as a weight you won't admit is there.

The grief may still surface. But you don't have to keep pretending there was nothing to lose.

Book 5 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You lost more than time. Let yourself feel it.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Too Many Tabs
books.subtitle: When everything is happening at once and your brain just locks up — finding the one thing under all of it
books.desc: Eleven tabs, three deadlines, four unread group chats — and a brain that responds to all of it by going completely blank. This is a book about the overwhelm that looks like laziness and is actually a flood.
books.long_desc:
There's too much. There's been too much for a while now.

The tasks have stacked into a wall. The messages keep coming. Everything is urgent and nothing is finished and somewhere in the middle of all of it your brain just — stopped. Went quiet. Picked the one thing that didn't matter, or picked nothing at all. From the outside it looks like you're avoiding. From the inside it feels like drowning while standing perfectly still.

This is a book for the mind that takes in everything at full volume and has no way to turn the inputs down. The one where ten open loops feel exactly as loud as each other, where a small task and a huge one weigh the same, where "just make a list" is laughable because the list is the thing crushing you. You're not lazy. You're flooded. There is a difference, and almost no one names it.

You'll learn to feel the flood as it rises — the static, the blank, the strange paralysis when there's the most to do — and to recognize freezing as something your overloaded system is doing for a reason, not a personal failing. You'll learn why the volume gets cranked, and what actually happens in your body when everything arrives at once.

Nothing here is a productivity system. No app, no method, no five-step morning routine that finally makes you on top of it. Those things assume the problem is that you haven't tried hard enough, and you have tried so hard. The work here is different: to find, underneath the roar of everything, the one real thing — and to let the rest be loud without obeying it.

The flood will still come. But you can learn to stand in it without going under.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Everything at once. So you freeze.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The Problem Is Me
books.subtitle: When anxiety stops feeling like something you have and starts feeling like something you are
books.desc: Somewhere along the way the anxiety stopped being about the moment and became a verdict about you. This is a book about the shame underneath it all — the quiet certainty that you're the thing that's broken.
books.long_desc:
Somewhere along the way, it stopped being about the thing that happened.

You missed the deadline, or froze in the meeting, or forgot the thing again — and the feeling that followed wasn't I made a mistake. It was something older and heavier: there's something wrong with me. Not a worry about the moment. A verdict about you. And you've heard it so many times, for so many years, that it doesn't even sound like an opinion anymore. It sounds like a fact.

This is a book for the person who has quietly decided they are the problem. The one who's been called too much and too sensitive and not enough, who's learned to read every anxious moment as more evidence of some core defect. You're not broken. You were handed a brain that runs hot and a world that kept telling you that was a personal failing — and somewhere in there, you started to believe it.

You'll learn to feel shame as a distinct thing — not the same as the anxiety, not the same as the truth — and to catch the exact moment a hard feeling curdles into a story about who you are. You'll learn where that story came from, why it feels so much like fact, and what it has quietly cost you to carry it this long.

Nothing here will fix you, because you were never the thing that needed fixing. That's not a slogan; it's the whole point. The shift is the slow, almost unbelievable recognition that you can have an anxious brain and a hard day and a long history of being misread — and still not be the problem you've spent years convinced you are.

The voice that says it's you may not go silent. But you can stop taking its word as the truth about who you are.

Book 4 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: When did it stop being a mistake and start being you?
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The Thought That Won't Let Go
books.subtitle: How one small worry becomes a three-hour spiral — and how to catch it before it pulls you all the way down
books.desc: It starts with one thought. Twenty minutes later you've replayed a conversation forty times and convinced yourself of the worst. This is a book about the spiral — how it pulls you in, and how to feel the first tug before it does.
books.long_desc:
It always starts so small.

One thought. A thing someone said. A reply that took too long. A mistake you might have made. And then the second thought hooks onto the first, and the third onto that, and twenty minutes later you've replayed the whole thing forty times, run every worst case, and arrived somewhere dark and certain — sure it all means something terrible about you. You didn't decide to go there. The spiral took you.

This is a book for the mind that can't leave a thought alone. The one that mistakes spiraling for problem-solving, that believes if it just thinks hard enough, long enough, it'll finally feel safe — and instead digs deeper every loop. You're not overthinking because you're weak. Your brain is doing what it's wired to do: chase the thread, find the threat, never stop. It's just that the chasing is the thing hurting you.

You'll learn to feel the spiral the moment it starts to pull — that first small tug downward, before the momentum takes over — and to recognize the loop as a loop instead of as the truth. You'll learn why your mind insists on running it, and what it actually costs you every time you go all the way down.

Nothing here will hand you the perfect thought to think your way out. There isn't one — that's the trap, the belief that the right answer is one more lap away. The shift is smaller and stranger than that: noticing you're in the spiral, and discovering you can step off the path instead of following it to the bottom.

The thoughts will still come and still try to link up. But you don't have to follow them all the way down anymore.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: One thought. Then a thousand.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Always Watching
books.subtitle: The part of you that monitors everything and never clocks out — and the surprising freedom in finally being able to watch it
books.desc: There's a part of you that scans every room, replays every face, monitors your own voice mid-sentence — always on, never off duty. This is a book about the watcher, and the unexpected calm in learning to watch it back.
books.long_desc:
There's a part of you that never clocks out.

It scans the room when you walk in. It reads every face for the thing that's about to go wrong. It monitors your own voice mid-sentence, checks your expression, replays the conversation while you're still having it. It's been on guard so long you don't even notice it running — it just feels like being you. Exhausting, hyperaware, braced. Always watching, in case.

This is a book for the mind that learned, somewhere far back, that the only way to be safe was to never stop scanning. The one that's vigilant about other people and merciless about itself, that can't fully relax in a room or in its own skin because some part of it is always on lookout. You're not paranoid and you're not too much. Your wiring just decided long ago that watching everything was how you'd survive — and it never got the message that it could stand down.

You'll learn to notice the watcher itself — to feel the constant scanning as a thing your nervous system is doing, not the simple truth of how the world is — and to recognize how much of your energy has quietly gone to a lookout that never sleeps. You'll learn why it turned on, what it's been so afraid of, and what it's like to finally catch it in the act.

And here, in the last book, something turns. Because the part of you that can notice the watcher is not the watcher. There's a quieter you underneath the vigilance — the one that's been doing the noticing all along — and it doesn't have to scan, or brace, or guard. Nothing here switches the lookout off. But you can step back far enough to see it working, and discover you were never only the alarm. You were always the one who could watch it.

The watching may not fully stop. But you can finally watch it back — and rest in the part of you that was never on guard at all.

Book 7 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're always on guard. Even from yourself.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Everyone in This Room Is Doing Better Than You
books.subtitle: For the high-achiever who measures every win against the feed — meeting the comparison that swears you're the one falling behind
books.desc: Every promotion post, every peer who makes it look effortless, lands like proof you're behind. This is a book about the comparison that turns every room into a scoreboard you're already losing.
books.long_desc:
You open the app to kill thirty seconds and close it feeling further behind than when you started.

Someone you graduated with just announced the role. Someone two years younger is speaking on the panel. The group chat is celebrating a win that makes yours feel small, and somewhere in the scroll a quiet verdict lands: everyone else figured out the thing you keep missing. They make it look easy. You're the one faking the part, ranked last in a race nobody else seems to know they're running.

This book is for the mind that turns every room into a scoreboard. The one wired to scan, to rank, to clock who's ahead before you've even said hello — until the measuring turns inward and runs all day. You're not bitter and you're not behind. You've just aimed a fast, sharp instrument at the wrong target: yourself, against a feed of other people's best ten seconds.

Chapter by chapter, you'll learn to catch comparison as it rises — the reflexive ranking, the scroll that leaves you smaller, the certainty that everyone else got the manual you didn't. You'll see how the very wiring that makes your brain feel like a liability — the noticing, the reading of every room, the hunger to do it right — is also exactly what got you into the room in the first place. And you'll feel how little the comparison ever told you that was true.

Nothing here will help you win it. That's the point. There is no ranking that finally settles it, no version of you impressive enough to come out on top of a feed that never ends. There is only the slow, steadying recognition that the highlight reels were never the whole story — and that the room you're standing in is one you earned a place in.

The comparing mind may still speak. But it doesn't get to set the terms of the room.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Their highlight reel is not your evidence.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: One Email From Being Found Out
books.subtitle: For the high-achiever who's sure they faked their way in — meeting the shame that swears you don't belong
books.desc: You're sure that any day now, someone will realize they let the wrong person in. This is a book about the shame that runs under every win, whispering that you don't actually belong here.
books.long_desc:
There's a particular kind of quiet that comes after good news. The promotion lands, the praise comes in, the offer is real — and instead of relief, your stomach drops. Because now there's further to fall when they find out.

You know the feeling. The replayed sentence. The certainty that you got away with something. The exhausting math of staying one step ahead of being seen, because if anyone looked too closely they'd discover what you already believe — that you faked your way in, that the real ones make it look easy, that you're a fraud who happens to be good at hiding it.

This book is for the mind that turns every success into evidence against itself. The one that over-delivers and over-prepares and answers emails at midnight, not because the work demands it, but because the shame does — that hot, sinking sense of being fundamentally not enough, dressed up well enough to pass.

Chapter by chapter, you'll learn to catch shame in the act: the flush, the urge to disappear, the story that you of all people don't deserve to be here. You'll see how the very things that make your brain feel like a liability — the hyperfocus, the masking, the way you read a room for the exit — are also exactly how you got this good. And you'll feel, maybe for the first time, how little the shame ever had to do with the truth.

Nothing here will make you more impressive. That's not the point. There is no performance that finally earns your spot, no version of you polished enough to silence it for good. There is only the slow, steadying recognition that you were never the imposter — and that you're allowed to stop bracing for the knock at the door.

The shame may still rise. But it doesn't get to decide whether you belong.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You didn't fool anyone. You earned it.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: Probably Fine
books.subtitle: For the mind that sounds the alarm when nothing's actually wrong
books.desc: That jolt of certainty that you messed something up — the reread message, the replayed meeting, the gut-drop over nothing — is a false alarm. This is a book about learning to tell the smoke from the fire.
books.long_desc:
You send the message. Then you reread it. Then you reread it again, and somewhere around the fourth pass your stomach drops, because what if it sounded off, what if they're annoyed, what if you just quietly ruined something.

Nothing happened. You know nothing happened. And your body is still bracing like it did.

This is the false alarm — the part of your mind that sounds the danger signal when there's no danger, that reads a one-word reply as a verdict and a quiet afternoon as proof something's wrong. It's not weakness and it's not paranoia. It's a smoke detector that goes off when you make toast. Loud, fast, and almost always wrong about the size of the threat.

You're not going to learn to switch it off. You can't, and honestly the people who tell you to have never lived in a head like yours. What you can learn is to recognize the alarm for what it is — the specific feeling of it, the way it lands before any actual thought does, the stories it reaches for every single time. Once you can name it, it stops getting to drive.

Chapter by chapter, you'll get to know your own false alarm up close: where it fires, what it's protecting, why it's so sure and so often wrong. You'll feel the difference between something that needs you and something that just feels like it does. Not to win against your mind. To stop trusting it on reflex.

The alarm will probably still go off. Toast is toast. But you can learn to glance at it, notice there's no fire, and go back to your night.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your brain cried wolf. Again.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: One Thought Becomes Forty
books.subtitle: For the mind that starts with one worry and ends up rewriting your whole life by midnight
books.desc: It starts with one small thing and somehow ends with you questioning your whole life. This is a book about the spiral — how one thought recruits the next, and how to feel it pick up speed before it takes the night.
books.long_desc:
It always starts small. A text that went unanswered. A thing you said that landed weird. One loose thread.

And then you pull it. Just to check. And the next thought is already loading before you finish the first one — and the one after that — and forty minutes later you're not thinking about the text anymore, you're somehow questioning your job, your friendships, whether you're actually okay as a person. One worry walked in and brought everyone it knows.

This is the spiral. Not a single thought you can argue with, but a chain reaction — each link handing off to the next, faster and faster, until you're three tabs deep into a worst case you didn't even mean to open. It feels like thinking. It feels productive, almost, like if you just follow it far enough you'll finally hit the bottom and know. You never hit the bottom. That's the whole trick of it.

You're not going to learn to never spiral again. The spiral is fast and it's old and it doesn't ask permission. What you can learn is to feel the moment it goes from one thought to the loop — the shift in your body, the speed picking up, the specific flavor of certainty that means you've left the actual problem behind and started free-falling. That moment is where everything changes.

Chapter by chapter, you'll get to know your own spiral up close: where it tends to start, how it gathers speed, the stories it always reaches for on the way down. You'll feel the difference between a thought you're having and a thought that's having you. Not to win the descent. To notice you've stepped off the edge, and to find you're allowed to stop pulling the thread.

The first thought will still come. They always do. But you can learn to catch it before it becomes forty, and let the rest of the night stay yours.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: It never stops at the first thought.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: The One Who Watches You
books.subtitle: For the mind that narrates every move you make and grades you on all of it
books.desc: There's a part of you watching you live — narrating the conversation while you're in it, grading how you came across, replaying the tape after. This is a book about the watcher, and what happens when you finally turn and look back.
books.long_desc:
You said the thing, and even as you were saying it, someone was watching you say it.

Narrating it. That came out wrong. They noticed. You're doing the thing again. There's a part of you that never quite joins the room — it hangs back, takes notes, runs the commentary while you're still in the middle of living. And later, when you're finally alone, it plays the whole tape back and grades you on it. Frame by frame.

This is the watcher. Not your thoughts, exactly — the thing that watches your thoughts. The inner camera that's always on, the voice that turns every ordinary moment into footage to be reviewed. It tells you it's keeping you safe, making sure you don't mess up, catching the mistake before anyone else does. What it actually does is make sure you never get to just be in your own life. You're always slightly outside it, watching.

You're not going to learn to shut the watcher off. It's been on a long time, and it's convinced it's the only reason you've held it together. What you can learn is to feel the difference between living a moment and surveilling it — to notice when you've drifted up out of your own experience to go stand behind the camera again. And then, slowly, to wonder who the watcher is even performing for.

Chapter by chapter, you'll get to know the one who watches you up close: when it switches on, what it's so afraid will happen if it ever blinks, the grades it's been handing out that you never agreed to be graded by. You'll feel what it's like to be inside a moment instead of reviewing it. Not to defeat the watcher. To turn around, finally, and look at it.

The commentary may still run. It's been running a while. But you can learn to step back into your own life while it does — and to find that you were always allowed to simply be here, unwatched, ungraded, home.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Someone in your head is taking notes.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: Everyone Else Has It Figured Out
books.subtitle: For the mind that measures itself against the feed — and always somehow comes up short
books.desc: Everyone on your feed seems further ahead, calmer, more sorted than you. This is a book about comparison — the ranking that runs all day and never once tells you the truth.
books.long_desc:
Everyone else seems to have it figured out.

They've got the internship, the friend group that actually does things, the skin, the GPA, the relationship, the five-year plan. You watch it scroll past — the launches, the soft-launch couples, the "so blessed" captions — and somewhere in your chest the math starts running automatically: they're ahead, you're behind, and the gap is your fault. By the time you put the phone down you feel vaguely sick and can't quite say why.

This book is for the mind that ranks. The one that turned looking at other people into a competition you never agreed to enter and somehow always lose. You're not shallow for feeling it and you're not failing at life. You've just been handed an endless feed of everyone's best angle and asked to compare it, in real time, to the inside of your own head — which is a fight rigged from the start.

You won't find advice here about deleting the apps or being grateful for what you have. The problem was never that you don't appreciate your life. The problem is that you keep measuring it against a highlight reel and calling the result the truth.

What you'll learn instead is how to catch the comparison as it rises — the reflexive ranking, the certainty that everyone else is fine — and to feel how little it has ever actually told you. The calm you imagine in other people is a story you're writing from the outside. The "figured out" you think you're missing was never the thing making you anxious.

The feed will keep scrolling. You just don't have to keep score against it.

Book 6 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're comparing your behind-the-scenes to their highlight reel.
books.author: Nico Crane
keywords: everyone else has it figured out

books.title: Nothing's Wrong and I Can't Breathe
books.subtitle: For when your body sounds the alarm and there's no actual fire — making sense of panic that shows up out of nowhere
books.desc: Your heart is racing, your chest is tight, and nothing is actually happening. This is a book about the false alarm — anxiety that fires when there's no real danger, and how to stop treating it like there is.
books.long_desc:
Your heart is pounding. Your hands won't sit still. Some deep part of you is absolutely certain that something is wrong — and you cannot, for the life of you, find the thing.

You're sitting in a lecture, or walking across the quad, or lying in bed at a perfectly normal hour, and your body hits the panic button anyway. No tiger. No exam tomorrow. No reason. Just the alarm, screaming, while the rest of you stands there going: what? What is it? What did I miss?

Here's the thing nobody explains. Your body isn't broken. It's overprotective. It learned somewhere along the line to treat a tight deadline like a predator, a weird text like a threat, a quiet Tuesday like the calm before something terrible — and now it sounds the alarm whether or not there's a fire. That racing heart isn't a warning you have to solve. It's a smoke detector going off because you made toast.

You won't find breathing tricks here that promise to switch the alarm off in sixty seconds. You'll find something more useful: a way to recognize the false alarm for what it is. To feel the surge rise and think, oh — it's this again, the body crying wolf — instead of scrambling to figure out what's wrong with you.

Because the panic was never proof that you're in danger. It was just proof that the alarm works a little too well. And once you can see that, the whole thing gets quieter — not because you forced it down, but because you stopped feeding it.

The alarm might still go off. You just don't have to come running every time.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your body is lying to you, kindly.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: It Hits at the Weirdest Times
books.subtitle: For grief that won't stay in the lines — loss that ambushes you in a dining hall, a song, an ordinary Tuesday
books.desc: Grief doesn't follow the stages. It hits in a dining hall, a song, a random Tuesday. This is a book about loss that keeps changing shape — and the anxiety of never knowing when it's coming.
books.long_desc:
It got you in the dining hall. Of all places. A song you weren't even listening to, a smell, a flash of something — and suddenly you're not okay, in line for waffles, blinking hard so nobody sees.

Grief was supposed to be tidier than this. Stages, a timeline, a place where it lives and stays. But yours doesn't follow the script. It went quiet for two weeks and then knocked you flat over nothing. It shows up at parties and skips the funeral. It changes shape — sharp one day, hollow the next, gone long enough that you feel guilty, then back like it never left. And underneath it all hums a low anxiety: when is it coming next, and will I be somewhere I can't fall apart?

This book won't walk you through the five stages or promise that it gets smaller on a schedule. Grief isn't a process you complete. It's a thing that moves through you in its own time, in its own weird order, and there is nothing broken about you for grieving sideways.

What you'll learn instead is how to recognize the ambush for what it is — not a setback, not proof you're doing it wrong, but grief changing shape again — and how to let it move through you without bracing against the next wave every waking minute. The dread of when-will-it-hit loosens not when the grief ends, but when you stop treating each wave as an emergency.

The loss won't follow the lines. You just don't have to stand guard against it to get through your day.

Book 5 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: It never warns you when it's coming.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Too Much, All at Once
books.subtitle: For when there's so much to do that you do nothing at all — understanding the shutdown that looks like laziness
books.desc: The to-do list is so long you froze. The more there is to do, the less you can start. This is a book about overwhelm — the shutdown that looks like laziness and isn't.
books.long_desc:
There's so much to do that you're doing absolutely nothing.

You know how this goes. The assignments pile up. The emails. The texts you meant to answer three days ago. The thing your group project needs. And instead of getting faster, you get slower — until you're lying on your bed staring at the ceiling while the whole list just sits there, glowing, on a closed laptop you can't bring yourself to open.

From the outside it looks like you don't care. You're "behind." You're "procrastinating." You've probably called yourself lazy more times than you can count. But that's not what this is. This is overwhelm — the specific kind of freeze that happens when there's too much coming in and your brain quietly pulls the plug rather than catch fire. It's not a character flaw. It's a circuit breaker.

This book won't hand you a color-coded planner or a productivity system that assumes the problem is your time management. The problem was never that you don't know how to make a list. The problem is that the list became a wall, and walls don't respond to better handwriting.

Instead, you'll learn to recognize the shutdown as it's happening — to feel the input climb past what you can hold and notice the freeze for what it is, instead of piling shame on top of paralysis. You'll see that the smallest possible movement is not a failure to do the big thing. It's the only thing that ever actually works.

The list will probably still be long tomorrow. But "too much" stops being a verdict on you — and starts being a feeling you know how to meet.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: When everything matters, nothing moves.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: If They Really Knew Me
books.subtitle: For the secret fear that you're not enough underneath it all — and the exhausting work of hiding it
books.desc: Underneath the anxiety is a quieter fear: that if people saw the real you, they'd leave. This is a book about shame — the work of hiding, and what happens when you set it down.
books.long_desc:
There's a fear underneath the other fears. The one you don't say out loud.

It's the quiet certainty that you're somehow less than everyone around you. That you got into the room by accident. That you're pulling off some elaborate trick, and one day the right person will look closely and see it — the realness of you, the not-enough of you — and that'll be that. So you manage. You curate. You laugh at the right moments and keep the rest hidden and stay exhausting-vigilant about the gap between who you are and who you're letting people see.

That's shame. Not the loud kind — the structural kind, the kind that runs so deep you mistake it for a fact about yourself. And it's quietly running a huge amount of your anxiety, because hiding is a full-time job and the dread of being found out never fully clocks off.

This book won't try to talk you into self-love or convince you to "just be confident." You can't affirmation your way out of something this old, and you know it. Pretending the shame isn't there is just one more thing to hide.

What you'll learn instead is how to recognize shame for what it is — a feeling that arrived a long time ago and started impersonating the truth. You'll see the difference between the real you and the verdict you've been carrying about the real you. And you'll find that the thing you've been frantically covering was never the problem. The covering was.

The fear might still whisper that you're not enough. You just don't have to organize your whole life around keeping it secret.

Book 4 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: The mask is heavier than the secret.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: The Same Thought, Forty Times
books.subtitle: For the mind that replays everything on a loop — getting out of the thought spiral without arguing your way out
books.desc: One thought, looping. The same scene, replayed. The same worry, rerun until 3am. This is a book about the spiral — and how to step off it without winning the argument.
books.long_desc:
It's the same thought. Again. For the fortieth time tonight.

Maybe it's something you said in class that landed weird. Maybe it's a text that hasn't been answered. Maybe it's nothing you can even name — just a loop, running and running, the same scene replayed from every angle, each lap somehow making it feel more urgent and less solvable at the same time. You know you're spiraling. Knowing doesn't stop it. That almost makes it worse.

Here's what no one tells you about the spiral: you cannot think your way out of it. The loop isn't a problem your mind is trying to solve. The loop is the mind solving nothing, on repeat, because somewhere it decided that if it just goes around one more time, it'll finally feel safe. It never does. The thirtieth lap feels exactly like the first.

This book won't teach you to argue with the thought, debunk it, or replace it with something positive — because you've tried that, at 3am, and the spiral just absorbs the argument and keeps going. Fighting a loop is how you stay in it.

What you'll learn instead is how to recognize the spiral as a spiral — to feel the pull of the next lap and notice, this is the loop, not the truth — and to let the thought keep circling without climbing on board every time. The loop loses its grip not when you defeat it, but when you stop treating each lap like new information.

The thought might still come back. You just don't have to ride it all the way around anymore.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You already know how this ends.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Watching My Own Life from the Back Row
books.subtitle: For when you go numb and float above it all — the strange quiet of being there without being there
books.desc: You're in the room but not really in it — watching your own life happen from somewhere just behind your eyes. This is a book about the numbness, the floating, the going-flat that anxiety leaves behind.
books.long_desc:
You're in the room. You're just not really in it.

The conversation is happening. Your mouth is making the right responses. And you're somewhere a few feet back, watching it all unfold like a video of yourself — the colors a little flat, the sound a little far away, the whole day running through a pane of glass you can't get to the other side of. People ask if you're okay and you say yeah, because how do you explain that you're not sad exactly, you're just not here.

This is the part of anxiety nobody warns you about. After the racing and the spiraling and the bracing, sometimes the body just... pulls the shutters. Goes numb. Floats you up and out so you don't have to feel the thing it can't handle. It looks like calm from the outside. From the inside it's its own kind of lonely — being technically present for your own life and somehow missing it.

This book won't tell you to "stay present" or shame you for checking out, because the checking out was never a choice you made. It was protection. Your system learned that going far away was safer than staying, and it got good at it.

What you'll learn instead is how to recognize the floating for what it is — not coldness, not brokenness, but a nervous system that took you somewhere safe — and how to find your way gently back into the room without forcing it. The glass thins not when you fight your way through it, but when you stop treating your own numbness as one more thing wrong with you.

The shutters might still come down sometimes. You just don't have to be afraid you've lost the way back.

Book 7 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Present, technically. Here, not really.
books.author: Nico Crane

books.title: Everyone Else Got the Manual
books.subtitle: For when the whole class seems to have received instructions you missed — and the feed proves it every time you scroll
books.desc: It feels like everyone else got a manual you never received — they know how to do this, how to belong, how to be fine — and every scroll through the feed confirms it. This is a book about the comparison that ranks you out of the room.
books.long_desc:
Everyone else seems to have gotten the manual. They know when to email the professor and what to say. They have the friends, the internships, the ease. They post the lecture-hall photos and the little wins and the casual brilliance, and you read it all as evidence: there were instructions, and you're the only one who never got them.

So you rank. Constantly, automatically. Smarter than you, more put-together than you, further ahead than you. Every seminar is a leaderboard, every scroll a fresh scoreboard, and somehow you're always near the bottom — the one who snuck in without the manual everyone else seems to be reading from.

This book is for the brain that can't stop measuring. The fast, comparison-wired, ADHD-shaped one that benchmarks you against every peer in the room and every highlight on the feed, and reads the gap as proof you don't belong. You're not behind in a race. You're comparing your raw, unedited inside to everyone else's posted outside.

You'll learn to catch comparison as it fires — the reflexive ranking, the certainty everyone else is fine. To feel how little it's ever actually told you that was true. To remember that the people who look like they got the manual are, mostly, just better at not showing you the 2am — the same 2am you're living right now.

Nothing here will help you win the ranking. That's the whole point. There's no leaderboard, no manual, no one keeping the score you're so sure you're losing. There's only the slow recognition that you've been grading yourself against a fiction — and you're allowed to put the scorecard down.

Everyone else is winging it too. The manual was never real.

Book 6 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: They look like they know. You can't see their 2am.
books.author: Esme Thorne
keywords: not as good as my peers

books.title: The Email That Never Comes
books.subtitle: For when your brain keeps bracing for the moment they find out you don't belong — and the moment never arrives
books.desc: Some part of you is always waiting for the email that says it was all a mistake — that you got in by accident and they're about to notice. This is a book about the fraud-alarm that fires when nothing is actually wrong.
books.long_desc:
There's an email you're always half-waiting for. The one that says there's been a mistake. That you don't actually belong here, you slipped through somehow, and now they know.

It never comes. But your body doesn't seem to believe that. You walk into a seminar and your heart is already going. Someone asks a normal question and your face goes hot like you've been caught. The whole nervous system lights up like there's a threat in the room — and you look around, and there's nothing there. Just a desk, a deadline, people who aren't even looking at you.

This book is for the brain that sounds the alarm when there's no fire. The ADHD-wired one that floods fast, reads danger into a raised eyebrow, and treats "I'm not sure" as proof of fraud. You're not broken and you're not faking. Your threat-detector is just sensitive, fast, and currently pointed at the wrong thing — you.

You'll learn to feel the false alarm as it rises, in the body, before the story catches up. To notice the gap between the panic and the actual moment, which is almost always quieter than the dread. To stop treating a spike of fear as evidence of anything except that your alarm went off again.

Nothing here will make the feeling never happen. That's not the deal. The deal is that you stop confusing the alarm for the truth — and let it ring without packing your bags every time.

The email isn't coming. You can stop refreshing.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: The alarm is loud. The danger isn't real.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: The One I Was Supposed to Be
books.subtitle: For the quiet grief of the student you thought you'd be by now — and learning to be the one who actually showed up
books.desc: There's a version of you that you were sure you'd be by now — together, effortless, the real deal — and some ache that won't name itself is grief for them. This is a book about mourning the self you never got to be.
books.long_desc:
You had a picture of who you'd be by now. The student who reads ahead, who speaks up easy, who isn't scared of office hours — the one who belongs here without having to wonder. And somewhere along the way it became clear you're not them, and might not become them, and there's a grief in that you don't quite have permission to feel.

It shows up sideways. A flatness after a small win. A sting when someone makes it look effortless. A heaviness on an ordinary afternoon you can't trace to anything. It's not sadness about a thing that happened — it's mourning a thing that didn't. The person you were supposed to be, who turned out not to be coming.

This book is for that quiet, shapeless grief underneath the imposter feeling — the loss of the imagined self you measured yourself against. It speaks to the ADHD-shaped version of it: a childhood of "so much potential" that became a private funeral for the effortless person you were promised you'd grow into.

You'll learn to recognize grief when it wears other clothes — as numbness, as irritation, as a low fog with no obvious cause. To let yourself actually mourn the version that never existed, instead of either chasing them forever or pretending you don't miss them. To make room, slowly, for the one who's actually here — the one who showed up scared and stayed.

Nothing here will resurrect the person you thought you'd be. That's the hard, honest center of it. The point is to stop being haunted by them — and to let the real you, the one who's been doing the work all along, finally take up the space they were holding.

The one you were supposed to be isn't coming. The one who's here already did.

Book 5 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're mourning a person who never existed.
books.author: Esme Thorne
