books.title: Too Many Tabs Open
books.subtitle: For when there's so much to do that your brain just stops — and the freeze becomes one more reason to call yourself a fraud
books.desc: There's so much to do that your brain quietly closes the whole thing down — and then you sit there, frozen, sure that a real student would've started hours ago. This is a book about the overwhelm that looks like not trying.
books.long_desc:
Everything is due. Three readings, two problem sets, an email you've been scared to open for a week. You can feel the whole pile at once — and somewhere in feeling all of it, your brain just stops. Tabs open, nothing happening. You, staring.

And then the worst part starts: the story about what the freeze means. A real student would be working. Everyone else is working. You're the one who can't even begin, which must be proof you don't belong here — you got in by luck and now the work is calling your bluff.

This book is for the brain that takes in too much, all at once, and shuts the panel down to protect itself. The ADHD-wired one with no working volume knob, where ten things at medium become a wall of static you can't push through. The freeze isn't laziness. It's an overloaded system tripping its own breaker.

You'll learn to recognize overwhelm in the body before you've named it — the heaviness, the buzzing blank, the way time goes strange. To tell the difference between "I won't" and "there is too much input for one nervous system to hold." To find the one small edge of the pile that your brain can actually pick up, instead of trying to lift all of it and dropping everything.

Nothing here will make you a person who never freezes. The point isn't to defeat the shutdown. It's to stop reading it as a verdict on whether you're real — and to meet it as a signal that the load got too big, which is a problem you can actually work with.

The pile is loud. The shutdown is your brain, not your character.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: It's not laziness. It's a system overload.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: The Cover Story
books.subtitle: For when you've built a whole performance to hide the version of you that you're sure isn't enough — and you're exhausted from holding it up
books.desc: You've got a version of you that you show people — the one who's fine, on top of it, competent — and a private certainty that if they saw underneath, it'd be over. This is a book about the shame that makes you cover.
books.long_desc:
There's the you that you show, and the you that you hide. The first one answers "how's it going" with "good, busy, you?" The second one is the reason your stomach drops when someone gets too close, too kind, too curious — because some part of you is sure that underneath the performance is something they'd recoil from. Something not enough. Something wrong at the root.

So you cover. You laugh it off, change the subject, keep the surface smooth and the door shut. And it works, mostly. But holding a cover story up all day is its own kind of exhausting, and the loneliness of it is specific: surrounded by people, none of whom you'll let actually see you.

This book is for the shame underneath the imposter feeling — the quiet conviction that you, specifically, are the flawed one, and that being known would mean being found out. It speaks to the ADHD-shaped version: a lifetime of "you're not trying hard enough" that hardened into "something is wrong with me," and a whole personality built to hide it.

You'll learn to feel shame as a sensation in the body — the heat, the shrink, the urge to disappear — instead of as a fact about your worth. To notice the moment you reach for the cover, and to see what it costs. To let one true thing be seen, in small, survivable doses, and feel that the catastrophe you brace for doesn't come.

Nothing here will require you to rip the mask off and stand exposed. That's not healing, that's just a different panic. The point is gentler: to stop believing the thing under the cover is unlovable — and to need the cover a little less.

You are not the flaw you've been hiding. You're the one who got tired of hiding it.

Book 4 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: The mask is heavy. Underneath isn't the problem.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: Replay
books.subtitle: For when your brain runs the same five seconds on a loop all night — the thing you said, the look they gave, the proof you don't belong
books.desc: One thing you said. One look someone gave. And your brain runs it on a loop until 3am, each pass louder, each pass more certain it's proof. This is a book about the spiral that replays you to yourself.
books.long_desc:
It's one moment, usually. Something you said in the seminar that landed weird. A pause that went a beat too long. The face someone made that you can't stop seeing. And now your brain has it on a loop, playing it back, again, again, each pass adding a little more certainty that everyone noticed, everyone knows.

By midnight the five seconds have become a whole case against you. You weren't just awkward — you exposed yourself, confirmed the thing you're always afraid of, gave them the proof. You replay it looking for an exit and only ever find more evidence, because that's what the loop is built to do.

This book is for the brain that won't stop reviewing. The fast, sticky, ADHD-wired one that catches on a moment and circles it, mistaking the spinning for thinking, the replay for figuring it out. You're not solving anything in there. You're just running footage no one else even saved.

You'll learn to catch the spiral as it starts — the pull back to the same scene, the body winding tighter with every lap. To feel the difference between actually processing something and just rotating it. To step out of the loop without having to win it, because the loop was never going to reach a verdict that let you rest.

Nothing here will give you a brain that never circles. The point isn't to never replay. It's to notice you're in the loop sooner, and to stop treating the hundredth pass as if it's finally found the truth — when all it's found is more spin.

The footage will keep wanting to play. You don't have to keep watching.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your brain is reviewing footage no one else kept.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: Watching From the Back of the Room
books.subtitle: For when you're going through the motions of your own life from somewhere outside it — present, functioning, and strangely not there
books.desc: You're in the lecture, the conversation, your own life — and also somehow watching it from a few feet back, behind glass, going through the motions while feeling almost nothing. This is a book about the numbness that pulls you out of the room.
books.long_desc:
You're in the seminar, nodding at the right moments. You're in the conversation, saying the lines. You're living your life — and at the same time you're watching it happen from a few feet behind your own eyes, like you stepped out and left someone competent running the body. Present, functioning, and strangely not there.

It can feel safer back there. Behind the glass, the imposter fear gets muffled, the stakes go soft, nobody can really get to you. But it's lonely in a way that's hard to explain, and a little frightening — like you're missing your own life as it goes by, watching the footage instead of being in the scene.

This book is for the checked-out, faded-back, dissociated version of the imposter feeling — the numbness your nervous system reaches for when being fully present feels like too much exposure. It speaks to the ADHD-shaped overwhelm underneath it: a system that, flooded too many times, learned to handle "too much" by quietly leaving the room.

You'll learn to notice when you've drifted back behind the glass — the flatness, the autopilot, the sense of distance from your own hands. To find the gentlest way back in through the body, a few seconds at a time, without flooding the system that left for a reason. To be a little more here, a little more often, on terms your nervous system can actually tolerate.

Nothing here will demand you be fully, vividly present all the time. The watcher showed up to protect you, and you don't heal by forcing it out. The point is softer: to stop drifting out by default — and to choose, in small survivable doses, to come back into your own life.

You've been watching from the back. There's a seat for you up front when you're ready.

Book 7 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're in the room. You're also not.
books.author: Esme Thorne

books.title: Everyone Else Has It Figured Out
books.subtitle: For the mind that ranks itself against everyone's highlight reel — putting down the scorecard nobody else is even keeping
books.desc: Everyone on your feed seems to have it together — the internship, the friends, the life you're somehow behind on. This is a book about the comparison that runs every time you open your phone, and the scorecard nobody else is even keeping.
books.long_desc:
You open your phone to take a break and close it feeling worse. Someone got the internship. Someone's already got their life together at twenty. Someone looks effortless in a way you have never once felt, and somewhere in the scroll you've quietly decided you're losing a race you didn't know you'd entered.

It runs all day, this ranking. Who's ahead, who's behind, where you fall. You walk into class and clock who seems to get it faster. You measure your messy, real, behind-the-scenes life against everyone else's edited best, and you lose every time, because of course you do — you're comparing your blooper reel to their highlight reel.

This book is for the mind that can't stop keeping score. You're not shallow and you're not jealous. You came of age with a thousand other lives in your pocket, updated constantly, each one a fresh chance to feel like you're not enough. Of course you compare. You were handed the perfect machine for it.

You won't learn to win the comparison — there's no version of you that finally ranks first and feels okay, because the scorecard always finds a new column. Instead, you'll learn to catch the ranking as it happens. To feel the small drop when you measure yourself against a stranger's Tuesday, and to recognize how little it has ever actually told you that was true.

You'll learn that the calm and the togetherness you imagine in everyone else is a story you're writing from the outside, with almost no information. And you'll learn that the race was never real — that nobody else is keeping the scorecard you're killing yourself to win.

Nothing here will make your feed less shiny or the comparing mind go quiet for good. It'll still whisper that you're behind. But you can stop letting it set the terms, and put down a scorecard that was only ever measuring you against a fiction.

Book 6 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Their feed looks like a life you're failing at.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: The Alarm With No Fire
books.subtitle: For the racing mind that's sure something's wrong — making peace with a body that sounds the alarm when nothing's actually burning
books.desc: Your heart's pounding, your thoughts are racing, you're certain something is wrong — and nothing is. This is a book about the false alarm your brain keeps pulling, and how to stop running from a fire that isn't there.
books.long_desc:
You know this feeling. The jolt at your desk for no reason. The certainty that something is wrong, sitting in a perfectly ordinary Tuesday. Your heart going like you're being chased across the quad when you're just sitting in the back of a lecture hall, trying to take notes.

Here's the thing nobody tells you: your body can't tell the difference between a real threat and a hard week. So it does the only thing it knows how to do. It pulls the alarm. Loud. Over and over. For a fire that was never there.

This book is for the mind that lives at high alert — that reads a quiet phone as a problem, a delayed text as a verdict, an ordinary day as a setup for something terrible. You're not dramatic and you're not broken. You're running a very sensitive alarm system in a world that keeps tripping it.

You won't learn to shut the alarm off. You'll learn to recognize it — to feel the surge rise and know it for what it is, an old reflex doing its loud, clumsy job. You'll learn that the dread is a feeling, not a forecast. That your body sounding off is not proof that anything is wrong.

Nothing here will make you fearless, and it won't pretend the alarm will never ring again. It will. But there's a difference between hearing the alarm and believing the building is on fire. This is a book about learning to stand in the noise and notice: there's no smoke. There's just you, alive and a little wired, in a room where nothing is actually burning.

Book 1 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Your body is yelling fire in an empty room.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: The Grief That Comes In Sideways
books.subtitle: For the mind that overthinks a loss it was supposed to be over — letting the missing change shape instead of forcing it to end
books.desc: It hits you in the dining hall, in a song, in a Tuesday you weren't braced for — a loss you were sure you'd moved past. This is a book about the grief that keeps changing shape, and the overthinking we use to try to make it behave.
books.long_desc:
You thought you were past this. Then a song came on in the dining hall, or you went to text someone who isn't there to text anymore, or it was just a regular Tuesday — and the loss arrived sideways, out of nowhere, as fresh as the first day.

And then comes the second wave, the one that's almost worse: the thinking about it. Shouldn't you be over this by now? Are you doing grief wrong? Why does it keep coming back when everyone acts like there's a finish line you should've crossed already?

This book is for the mind that overthinks its own grief. The loss might be a person, a version of yourself, a future you were promised, the way things used to be before everything changed. You're not stuck and you're not broken. You're carrying something that doesn't move in a straight line, and you've been told a story about grief that doesn't match how it actually lives in a body.

You won't be handed the stages, or a timeline, or a method to process it efficiently and be done. Grief doesn't end on schedule, and pretending it should is part of what's hurting. Instead, you'll learn to recognize the missing when it rises — to feel it move through without rushing to explain it, fix it, or apologize for it still being here.

You'll learn that grief changes shape rather than disappearing — that it comes in sideways precisely because it's looking for somewhere soft to land. And you'll learn that the overthinking, the constant am-I-doing-this-right, is just fear wearing a clipboard. There's nothing to grade. There's only what you lost, and the love that's still looking for somewhere to go.

Nothing here will make the missing stop. It won't, and a part of you wouldn't want it to. But you can stop fighting the shape it takes, and let it be what it is without turning it into one more thing to overthink.

Book 5 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You thought you were past this by now.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: Too Many Tabs Open
books.subtitle: For the mind that has too much input and shuts all the way down — finding the ground when everything is asking for you at once
books.desc: Everything is due, everyone needs something, and there's so much coming at you that you've gone completely still — staring at a wall, unable to start any of it. This is a book about the shutdown that looks like laziness and is actually too much.
books.long_desc:
There are too many tabs open. The real ones on your laptop, sure — but also the ones in your head. The email you haven't answered. The reading you're behind on. The text from your mom. The thing due Friday and the thing due Friday and the other thing due Friday. It's all there at once, all the time, and at some point your brain just — stops.

Not rests. Stops. You sit down to do one thing and you do nothing. You stare at the wall. You scroll without seeing. From the outside it looks like you don't care. From the inside, you care so much about so many things at the same time that you've gone completely offline.

This book is for the mind that doesn't get tired so much as flooded. The one that can hold maybe three things before the whole desktop freezes. You're not lazy and you're not behind because you're failing. You're behind because the world handed you forty things at once and expected you to feel calm about it.

You won't learn a productivity system here. There's no perfect planner that ends this. Instead, you'll learn to recognize the flood as it rises — the static, the heaviness, the sudden full-body no — and to understand that the shutdown is not a character flaw. It's what a thoughtful mind does when too much arrives at once.

You'll learn that you can't think your way out of a flood, but you can find one thing to stand on. Not all forty. One. You'll learn that the wall you're staring at is not the enemy, and that the stillness, when you stop fighting it, has something to teach you about how much you were carrying.

Nothing here will make the input stop coming. It won't. But you can stop believing the shutdown means something's wrong with you, and start meeting it as the signal it actually is.

Book 2 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: Forty things at once, so you froze.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: The Part You Don't Let Anyone See
books.subtitle: For the mind convinced that if they really knew you, they'd leave — loosening the quiet belief that something about you is wrong
books.desc: You perform okay. You keep it together in front of people. But underneath is a quiet certainty that if anyone saw the real you, they'd be gone. This is a book about that belief — and how much of your overthinking is built to hide it.
books.long_desc:
There's a version of you that you let people see, and there's the part you don't. The part that's sure you're behind, faking it, getting away with something. The part convinced that if your friends, your professors, the people you love actually saw what's underneath, they'd quietly back away.

So you manage it. You overthink every text so it lands right. You rehearse being normal. You cover, and you cover, and the covering is exhausting in a way you can't explain to anyone — because explaining it would mean letting them see.

This book is for the mind that runs on a quiet, ancient belief: that something about you, at the core, is wrong. Not a thing you did. You. You're not too sensitive and you're not making it up. You learned, somewhere, that you had to hide to be safe — and a lifetime of hiding turns into a lifetime of overthinking.

You won't be told to just love yourself. That advice has never once reached the place this lives. Instead, you'll learn to recognize the belief when it speaks — the flush of being found out, the urge to disappear, the certainty that you're the exception who doesn't get to be okay. You'll start to hear it as a voice you absorbed, not a fact you confirmed.

You'll learn that the part you hide is not the proof against you that it feels like. That the exhaustion of covering is not the price of being you — it's the price of believing you have to. And you'll begin, slowly, to question whether the thing you've spent years concealing was ever as unlovable as the belief insists.

Nothing here will hand you instant worth or make the old voice vanish. It'll still whisper. But you can stop taking it as gospel, and start letting a little more of the hidden part into the light without the world ending.

Book 4 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're sure they'd leave if they knew.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: The Same Eleven Seconds
books.subtitle: For the mind that replays the same moment on a loop — learning to step off the thought that keeps circling back
books.desc: One thing you said. One look someone gave you. And your mind has played it back a hundred times tonight, each loop a little worse. This is a book about the spiral — and how to step off the track without forcing the thought to stop.
books.long_desc:
It's the same eleven seconds. The thing you said in section that landed wrong. The look on their face. You've played it back so many times tonight you've started editing it, adding things, making it worse, and you can't stop, and it's 2am, and the loop just keeps going around.

You know it doesn't help. That's the cruel part. You know, somewhere, that replaying it for the hundredth time changes nothing — and you do it anyway, because the spiral promises that if you just think it through one more time, you'll finally feel okay. You never do. The loop just tightens.

This book is for the mind that circles. The one that mistakes rumination for problem-solving, that treats the same worn thought like it might crack open and release you. You're not obsessive and you're not weak. You have a mind that loops, and a looping mind is convinced, every single time, that the next lap is the one that helps.

You won't learn to force the thought to stop — anyone who's tried knows that telling a spiral to quit just feeds it. Instead, you'll learn to recognize the loop the moment you're in it. To feel the difference between thinking and circling. To notice the spiral's oldest lie: that more laps mean more control.

You'll learn that you don't have to win the argument with the thought. You can just stop showing up to it. Not by white-knuckling, but by recognizing the track for what it is — a groove your mind fell into, not a problem that needs one more pass.

Nothing here will give you a mind that never loops. It'll loop again. But you can learn to catch the spiral earlier, hold it more loosely, and step off the track long before it drags you all the way down.

Book 3 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You've replayed it a hundred times tonight.
books.author: Mae Meridian

books.title: Watching From Behind the Glass
books.subtitle: For the mind that drifts out of its own life and goes numb — finding the way back into a body that's gone quiet and far away
books.desc: You're in the room, in the conversation, in your own life — and somehow you're watching it all from behind glass, far away and faintly numb. This is a book about checking out, and the slow, gentle way back in.
books.long_desc:
You're sitting in the dining hall with your friends and you're not really there. You can see it happening — the laughing, the talking, your own face nodding along — but you're watching it from somewhere behind your eyes, a step removed, like a show about your life playing on a screen across the room.

People call it spacing out, but it's bigger than that. The world goes flat and far away. Your hands feel like they belong to someone else. You go through the whole day on autopilot and can't remember any of it, and the scariest part is that you don't even feel scared. You don't feel much of anything. You've gone somewhere quiet, and you're not sure how to get back.

This book is for the mind that checks out. The one that, when things get to be too much, doesn't spiral or panic — it leaves. Floats up and away and watches from a safe distance. You're not crazy and you're not empty. Going far away is something a mind learns to do when being fully present once felt like too much to survive.

You won't be told to just be present, as if you hadn't tried, as if the door back in were standing wide open. Instead, you'll learn to recognize the drift as it starts — the flattening, the glass coming down, the slow slide out of your own body. You'll learn that the numbness is not nothing; it's protection, doing the only job it knows.

You'll learn that the way back in is not forced but invited — small, real, through the body and the senses, one ordinary thing at a time. And you'll learn that the watcher behind the glass is not broken or gone. It's you, waiting at a safe distance, for it to feel okay to come back down.

Nothing here will keep you anchored every second or promise you'll never drift again. You will. But you can learn to notice when you've gone, and find your own quiet way back into the life that's still here, waiting on this side of the glass.

Book 7 of the Livewire Books series.
books.angle: You're here, but you're watching from outside.
books.author: Mae Meridian
